message.content:
## Web Searcher: alignment/timing — timestamps & forced aligners

Research question: "BEST LOCAL / OPEN-SOURCE TEXT-TO-SPEECH (TTS) MODELS TO REPLACE OR AUGMENT KOKORO for an English educational-podcast pipeline, as of 2026. Baseline to beat: Kokoro-82M (hexgrad/Kokoro-82M), currently run locally on Apple Silicon (CPU + MPS), producing 24kHz audio, multiple voices (af_heart/am_onyx/bf_isabella/bm_george etc.), American + British English, two-voice format (narrator + question voice). Evaluate candidates across these angles: (1) QUALITY/NATURALNESS — which open/local TTS models beat Kokoro on naturalness and intelligibility for English (candidates to assess: XTTS-v2/Coqui, Piper, StyleTTS2, F5-TTS, Fish-Speech/OpenAudio, Parler-TTS, MeloTTS, Orpheus-TTS, Chatterbox, Kitten-TTS, Zonos, and any newer 2025-2026 open models) — report MOS/quality benchmarks, license (commercial/redistribution OK?), and model size. (2) WORD/PHONEME-LEVEL TIMING & ALIGNMENT — which models natively output word/phoneme timestamps (durations), and what forced-alignment tools (Montreal Forced Aligner, WhisperX, aeneas, ctc-forced-aligner) best produce a text↔audio timing map from existing audio — this enables content-aware speed compression, script-highlighting, and a true text-to-audio map. (3) HIGH-SPEED COMPREHENSIBILITY — which model's output stays most intelligible under aggressive time-compression / at high playback speed (crisp consonants, clean formant transitions); also the best FORMANT/robotic option (eSpeak-NG, RHVoice, DECtalk-like) for an extreme 16x 'ultra' track. (4) MULTI-VOICE & VOICE CONTROL — voice variety, voice cloning, prosody/rate control, and ease of adding new voices for a narrator+question two-speaker format. (5) LOCAL-INFERENCE PRACTICALITY on Apple Silicon and CPU — real-time factor / batch render speed, RAM/VRAM needs, MPS/CoreML/ONNX support, latency, and integration effort to swap into a Python pipeline that today calls Kokoro. For EACH recommended model give: quality vs Kokoro, license, local-inference feasibility on a Mac, whether it emits alignment, and a clear verdict on whether it's worth switching to (or adding alongside Kokoro). Prefer authoritative/benchmark sources and recent (2025-2026) comparisons; flag where claims are vendor marketing vs independent evaluation."

Your angle: **alignment/timing — timestamps & forced aligners** — Covers which models emit native alignment and which forced-alignment tools best build a text-to-audio map for highlighting and speed compression (Angle 2).
Search query: `TTS word phoneme level timestamps duration output forced alignment Montreal Forced Aligner WhisperX ctc-forced-aligner aeneas text audio timing map`

## Task
Use WebSearch with the query above (or a refined version). Return the top 4-6 most relevant results.
Rank by relevance to the ORIGINAL question, not just the search query. Skip obvious SEO spam/content farms.
Include a short snippet capturing why each result is relevant.

Structured output only.

Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.

- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").

Links: [{"title":"Word-level timestamps from WhisperX are inaccurate compared to Montreal Forced Aligner (MFA) · Issue #1247 · m-bain/whisperX","url":"https://github.com/m-bain/whisperX/issues/1247"},{"title":"Multilingual Word-Level Forced Alignment with Self-Supervised Representations and Learned Dynamic Programming","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2606.10675"},{"title":"Whisper Has an Internal Word Aligner Sung-Lin Yeh∗, Yen Meng∗, Hao Tang","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2509.09987"},{"title":"Whisper Has an Internal Word Aligner","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2509.09987v1"},{"title":"Tradition or Innovation: A Comparison of Modern ASR Methods for Forced Alignment","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2406.19363v1"},{"title":"BFA: REAL-TIME MULTILINGUAL TEXT-TO-SPEECH FORCED ALIGNMENT","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2509.23147v1"},{"title":"(PDF) Speech-to-Text Forced Alignment Benchmark","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/396940298_Speech-to-Text_Forced_Alignment_Benchmark"},{"title":"Tradition or Innovation: A Comparison of Modern ASR Methods for Forced   Alignment","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2406.19363"}]

Based on the search results, here's what recent research shows about these forced alignment tools:

## Overall Performance Comparison

MFA outperforms MMS and WhisperX in the comparison of word-level alignments at all time resolutions. The MFA outperformed both WhisperX and MMS, revealing a shortcoming of modern ASR systems.

## Specific Accuracy Metrics

MFA achieves 72.8% word-level accuracy at 20ms tolerance on TIMIT and 69.9% on Buckeye, while WhisperX achieves only 52.7% word-level accuracy at 20ms tolerance on TIMIT.

## Key Technical Differences

The classical GMM-HMM architecture still outperforms simple adaptations of the modern end-to-end ASR systems for forced alignment. The HMM-based system of the MFA has a high temporal resolution of 10 msec relative to MMS and WhisperX that operate over longer stretches of audio.

## CTC-Based Approaches

WhisperX uses CTC to derive word boundaries, while other approaches are based on cross-attention maps in Whisper. Recent research also compares against CTC-based alternatives, with MMS CTC-based alignment and other systems evaluated against newer approaches, with some methods outperforming all baselines across datasets and evaluation thresholds.

The consensus from 2025 research is that while neural approaches like WhisperX show promise for speed and multilingual capability, the traditional Montreal Forced Aligner remains superior for pure alignment accuracy.

Links: [{"title":"Kokoro for TTS and word timestamps | Ryan Welch","url":"https://ryanwelch.co.uk/blog/kokoro-word-timestamps/"},{"title":"Individual Word Timestamps - Kokoro TTS · Issue #278 · KoljaB/RealtimeTTS","url":"https://github.com/KoljaB/RealtimeTTS/issues/278"},{"title":"How to run Kokoro TTS model on-device","url":"https://www.nimbleedge.com/blog/how-to-run-kokoro-tts-model-on-device/"},{"title":"onnx-community/Kokoro-82M-v1.0-ONNX-timestamped · Extracting timestamps","url":"https://huggingface.co/onnx-community/Kokoro-82M-v1.0-ONNX-timestamped/discussions/2"},{"title":"hexgrad/Kokoro-82M · Hugging Face","url":"https://huggingface.co/hexgrad/Kokoro-82M"},{"title":"README.md · hexgrad/Kokoro-82M at b13b1ae9368fa5951c0e1612ba854c33940a38ed","url":"https://huggingface.co/hexgrad/Kokoro-82M/blob/b13b1ae9368fa5951c0e1612ba854c33940a38ed/README.md"},{"title":"Kokoro-82M TTS — Full Voice List (50 voices, 10 languages, CoreML, iOS) | Soniqo","url":"https://soniqo.audio/guides/kokoro"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Kokoro TTS phoneme timestamps alignment output and word durations:

## Overview

Kokoro is an open-weight TTS model with 82 million parameters that delivers comparable quality to larger models while being significantly faster and more cost-efficient.

## Phoneme Timestamps and Alignment

In Python, Kokoro exposes timing-related data directly, while in JavaScript, the public ONNX streaming API gives you the text for each chunk and the audio for each chunk, but not the model's native alignment output.

You can derive chunk duration from audio length and sampling rate, and distribute that chunk's duration across its original characters, then merge those character spans back into words.

## Misaki Tokenizer

Kokoro's tokenizer, Misaki (based on G2P), leverages libraries like Numpy, NLTK and Spacy. Kokoro uses misaki, a G2P library.

Text is converted to phoneme tokens via a three-tier pipeline including dictionary lookup, suffix stemming, and BART G2P for out-of-vocabulary words.

## Alignment Output

The Alignment matrix represents token-to-frame duration as a mask.

Based on the web search results, here's a comprehensive overview of forced alignment tools and techniques for generating word and phoneme-level timestamps:

## Forced Alignment Tools

### Montreal Forced Aligner (MFA)

The Montreal Forced Aligner is a command line utility for performing forced alignment of speech datasets using Kaldi. As a forced alignment system, the Montreal Forced Aligner will time-align a transcript to a corresponding audio file at the phone and word levels provided there exist a set of pretrained acoustic models and a pronunciation dictionary of the words in the transcript with their canonical phonetic pronunciation(s). A major highlight of this system is the availability of pretrained acoustic models and grapheme-to-phoneme models for a wide variety of languages, as well as the ability to train acoustic and grapheme-to-phoneme models to any new dataset you might have.

### WhisperX

WhisperX adds forced phoneme alignment for sub-100ms word-level timestamps and pyannote-based speaker diarization. WhisperX runs the transcript through a wav2vec2 phoneme alignment model that maps each word to its precise audio position.

### CTC-Based Alignment (ctc-forced-aligner)

The ctc-forced-aligner provides atleast 5X less memory usage with an improved implementation, wide range of language support including English, Arabic, Russian, German, and 1126 more languages, and flexibility in alignment granularity allowing choice between aligning on a sentence, word, or character level. It integrates with Hugging Face's models leveraging pretrained Wav2Vec2, HuBERT, and MMS models for accurate alignment and provides output in JSON format.

### Aeneas

Aeneas uses the Sakoe-Chiba Band Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) algorithm to align the Mel-frequency cepstral coefficients (MFCCs) representation of the given audio wave and the audio wave obtained by synthesizing the text fragments with a TTS engine, and is a forced aligner that automatically associates to each text fragment the time interval in the audio file when that text fragment is spoken.

## TTS Applications

For TTS systems, phoneme-level timestamp alignment is equally important because TTS models require data on how long each sound is typically spoken to generate speech that sounds human-like, and without accurate phoneme timing, synthetic voices can sound rushed, robotic, or unnatural.

Timestamp alignment lets you retrieve timing information that matches the generated audio, which is useful for experiences like word highlighting, karaoke‑style captions, and lipsync.

Runs in-browser (WebGPU/WASM) or on local Node.js WebSocket/REST server (WebGPU/CPU). · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/met4citizen/HeadTTS"}]

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about the Kokoro-82M ONNX timestamped model and extracting word timestamps:

## Kokoro-82M ONNX Timestamped Model

Kokoro is a frontier TTS model for its size of 82 million parameters (text in/audio out).

### Word Timestamps Extraction

Users have discussed extracting timestamps for phonemes and words from the model, with modifications made to enable phoneme-level timestamps. 

To work with timestamps, you would have to split your sentence into tokens, which can be done with various NLP libraries. Misaki uses spacy to do this, with an approach involving loading an NLP model, processing text into tokens with text, tag, and whitespace information.

### Model Access

The implementation process involves converting input text to phonemes and mapping phonemes to IDs. The timestamped ONNX model is available on Hugging Face and can be downloaded from: https://huggingface.co/onnx-community/Kokoro-82M-v1.0-ONNX-timestamped/resolve/main/onnx/model.onnx.

There's a working implementation example available on GitHub that demonstrates phoneme-level timestamps, and it's possible to implement this in languages like C# where misaki (the phonemization tool) doesn't exist natively.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about these text-to-speech models and their duration prediction and phoneme alignment capabilities:

**Duration Predictors & Phoneme Alignment:**

Duration predictors help align input phonemes to output acoustic features by avoiding attention mechanism errors, a method that was introduced into TTS through non-autoregressive models like FastSpeech.

**StyleTTS2 Approach:**

StyleTTS2 uses flexible duration modules that predict per-phoneme duration probabilities and compute time-aligned attention weights via differentiable Gaussian convolution and softmax normalization. Model-predicted duration distributions are used for text-to-speech frame upsampling.

**XTTS & F5-TTS:**

The search results indicate these are primarily voice-cloning models. Piper is fastest on CPU, XTTS v2 has the best cloning quality, and F5-TTS clones from approximately 3 seconds with faster inference.

**Piper:**

While mentioned alongside XTTS and F5-TTS, the search results don't provide specific technical details about Piper's native duration predictor or output timestamp capabilities.

content.text: I now have comprehensive coverage of this angle. Let me compile the top results ranked by relevance to the original question.

results.title: MahmoudAshraf97/ctc-forced-aligner — Text-to-audio alignment using CTC forced alignment
results.snippet: Directly answers Angle 2. A practical, easy-to-integrate Python forced aligner built on Wav2Vec2/HuBERT/MMS that takes existing audio + text and emits sentence/word/character-level timestamps as JSON. Claims ~5X less memory than alternatives and supports 1100+ languages including English. This is the most pip-installable, lowest-friction option to build a text-to-audio timing map from Kokoro's existing 24kHz output for highlighting and content-aware speed compression — far simpler to wire into a Python pipeline than MFA (Kaldi).

results.title: Tradition or Innovation: A Comparison of Modern ASR Methods for Forced Alignment
results.snippet: Independent benchmark (not vendor marketing) directly comparing MFA vs WhisperX vs MMS for forced-alignment accuracy. Key finding: the classical GMM-HMM Montreal Forced Aligner still beats WhisperX/MMS at word-level alignment across all time resolutions, with MFA's 10ms temporal resolution vs the coarser windows of neural ASR aligners. Critical for deciding which aligner gives the most accurate text-audio map for tight speed compression and script highlighting.

results.title: WhisperX word-level timestamps inaccurate compared to Montreal Forced Aligner (MFA) — Issue #1247
results.snippet: Real-world corroboration of the benchmark above: reports MFA ~72.8% word accuracy at 20ms tolerance on TIMIT vs WhisperX ~52.7%. Useful caveat for the pipeline — WhisperX is fast and gives sub-100ms word timestamps plus diarization (handy for the two-voice narrator/question format), but for precise boundaries needed for aggressive time-compression, MFA is more reliable. Frames the speed-vs-accuracy tradeoff between the two main aligner choices.

results.title: onnx-community/Kokoro-82M-v1.0-ONNX-timestamped — Extracting timestamps (discussion)
results.snippet: Most baseline-specific result: a Kokoro ONNX variant that natively emits per-phoneme/per-token timestamps, plus discussion of how to split text into tokens (via Misaki/spaCy) and merge phoneme spans into words. Means the current Kokoro baseline can produce native alignment without a separate forced aligner at all — directly relevant to the 'whether it emits alignment' verdict and avoids re-running ASR over generated audio.

results.title: Kokoro for TTS and word timestamps — Ryan Welch
results.snippet: Hands-on walkthrough of getting word-level timestamps out of Kokoro: in Python, Kokoro exposes timing-related alignment data directly (token-to-frame duration mask), while the JS/ONNX streaming path only gives per-chunk text+audio, requiring you to distribute chunk duration across characters and merge back into words. Concrete integration guidance for adding script-highlighting/karaoke timing to the existing Kokoro Python pipeline on Apple Silicon.

results.title: met4citizen/HeadTTS — Neural TTS (Kokoro) with timestamps and visemes for lip-sync
results.snippet: Shows a production-style Kokoro wrapper that outputs word timestamps and visemes, running in-browser (WebGPU/WASM) or on a local Node.js server (WebGPU/CPU). Demonstrates that native Kokoro alignment is robust enough for lip-sync/highlighting use cases and gives a reference implementation for emitting a text-audio timing map without an external aligner — relevant to local-inference practicality and the alignment-output verdict for the baseline model.

content.text:
I've completed the research on the alignment/timing angle and returned the structured output. 

Summary of findings for the alignment/timing angle:

**Native TTS alignment (baseline Kokoro):** Kokoro can emit alignment without an external aligner. There's a dedicated `onnx-community/Kokoro-82M-v1.0-ONNX-timestamped` model, and in Python Kokoro exposes the token-to-frame duration mask directly. HeadTTS demonstrates production-grade word timestamps + visemes from Kokoro. This means the existing baseline already satisfies the text-to-audio map requirement for highlighting.

**Best forced aligners for an existing-audio timing map (ranked by fit):**
- `ctc-forced-aligner` (MahmoudAshraf97) — easiest Python integration, JSON word/char timestamps, low memory, the pragmatic choice to bolt onto the current pipeline.
- MFA — most accurate boundaries (~72.8% @ 20ms vs WhisperX ~52.7%), 10ms resolution, best for aggressive time-compression, but Kaldi-based and heavier to install.
- WhisperX — fast, sub-100ms word timestamps plus diarization (useful for the two-voice format), but independently shown less accurate than MFA.

The two key independent (non-vendor) sources are the arXiv "Tradition or Innovation" benchmark and the whisperX GitHub issue corroborating it.
